lesson_title: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.front: Does C++ provide built-in functionality to convert an enum to its string name?
cards.back: No. You must implement this yourself using switch statements, lookup tables, or third-party libraries.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.question: What technique does this use to convert enum to string?
cards.answer: A switch statement that maps each enumerator to its string name.
cards.explanation: This is a common pattern for enum-to-string conversion in C++.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: To output an enum as a string, you typically use a {{c1::switch statement}} or {{c2::lookup table}} to map values to names.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.front: What are two common approaches for converting enums to strings?
cards.back:
• Switch statement: Map each case to a string
  • Array lookup: Use enum value as index into string array
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.question: How does this array lookup approach work?
cards.answer: Uses enum value (1 for green) as array index. Prints "green".
cards.explanation: Array indices must match enum values. Only works if enums start at 0 and are contiguous.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.front: What is a risk of using array lookup for enum-to-string conversion?
cards.back: If enum values are not contiguous or don't start at 0, array indices won't match enum values, causing incorrect results or out-of-bounds access.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.explanation:
C++ lacks built-in enum reflection. Common solutions:
  • Switch statement (most reliable)
  • Array lookup (only if values are 0-based and contiguous)
  • Macro-based solutions
  • Third-party libraries (magic_enum)
Choose switch for safety.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.front: How would you convert a string to an enum value?
cards.back: Compare the string against known enum names (if/else chain or switch) and return the corresponding enum value. No built-in C++ support.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: When writing enum-to-string functions, use a {{c1::default}} case to handle {{c2::invalid}} or {{c3::unknown}} enum values.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.question: What's wrong with this array approach?
cards.answer: Out of bounds access. red=5 but array has only 3 elements (indices 0-2).
cards.explanation: Array lookup only works if enum values match array indices (0-based, contiguous).
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: To convert enum to string, you typically use a {{c1::switch}} statement that maps each enumerator to its {{c2::string name}}.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: Array lookup for enum-to-string only works if enum values are {{c1::contiguous}} and start at {{c2::0}}.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: C++ does {{c1::not}} provide built-in enum-to-string conversion; you must implement it with {{c2::switch}} or {{c3::lookup tables}}.
cards.source: 13.4 — Converting an enumeration to and from a string

cards.text: To convert enum to string, you typically use a {{c1::switch}} statement that maps each enumerator to its {{c2::string name}}.
